original_hypothesis.title: Fee Manipulation via Broken Access Control Enables Sell Lockout and Fund Trapping
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: Due to broken onlyManager modifier, anyone can call setMaxFeePercent followed by setExternalFeePercent to set total fees ≥ 100% (1 ether). When totalFee >= price, getSellPriceAfterFee underflows (price - totalFee), and _transferFees computes negative sellValue. Sellers cannot sell tokens, permanently trapping their funds in the bonding curve.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This combines the access control vulnerability with fee economics to create an irreversible fund-trapping scenario. Even after the fees are fixed, users who bought at high prices during manipulation may suffer losses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee — underflows if total fees >= price

preconditions: maxFeePercent has no hard upper bound vs 1 ether

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: setMaxFeePercent(2 ether) then setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether) → fees = 110%+ of price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Set fees > 100%, all sells revert with underflow, then extort users or wait
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Attacker buys at normal fees, sets fees to >100%, nobody can sell, then resets fees and sells at profit as only seller

economic_questions: Can the attacker also set protocolFeePercent to redirect buy fees to themselves while sells are blocked?
Is there a Solidity 0.8.7 checked arithmetic revert in _transferFees for the underflow?
validation_requirements: Set fees above 100% and confirm sell reverts
Verify no other bounds check prevents this configuration

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: All tokens become unsellable — total bonding curve TVL at risk
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Infinite if combined with fee destination redirection

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All token holders across all curves

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Confirm sell path reverts when totalFee > price

validation_result.mechanism: Fee manipulation via broken access control causing sell underflow
validation_result.impact_summary: Anyone can set total fees above 100%, causing all sell transactions to revert due to arithmetic underflow, permanently locking user funds in the bonding curve.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability stems from two critical flaws. First, the `onlyManager` and `onlyOwner` modifiers in `Security.sol` are broken; they evaluate a boolean expression (`msg.sender == owner;`) but do not include a `require` or `revert` statement, making the protected functions effectively public. Second, the fee configuration functions (`setMaxFeePercent`, `setExternalFeePercent`, `setProtocolFeePercent`) in `Curves.sol` do not enforce a hard upper bound of 1 ether (100%) on the total fee percentage. An attacker can call `setMaxFeePercent` with a value greater than 1 ether, and subsequently call `setExternalFeePercent` to set the sum of fee percentages above 100%. When any user attempts to sell their tokens via `sellCurvesToken`, the `_transferFees` function calculates the `sellValue` by subtracting all fees from the `price`. Because the total fees exceed the price, this subtraction underflows. Since the contract uses Solidity 0.8.7, the underflow triggers an automatic revert, completely blocking all sell transactions and trapping all user funds in the protocol.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The code evidence directly supports the hypothesis. `Security.sol` shows the defective modifiers. `Curves.sol` shows `setMaxFeePercent` lacks a 1 ether upper bound check. The `_transferFees` function clearly shows the subtraction `price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee` which will underflow if the sum of fee percentages exceeds 1 ether.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The modifier evaluates a boolean expression but does not revert if it is false, allowing any caller to bypass the access control.
There is no check to ensure `maxFeePercent_` is less than or equal to 1 ether (100%). Combined with the broken `onlyManager` modifier, anyone can set an arbitrarily high maximum fee.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet: uint256 sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee;
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: If the sum of the fee percentages exceeds 1 ether, the sum of the calculated fees will exceed the `price`. This subtraction will underflow, causing the transaction to revert in Solidity 0.8.7.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: 1. Attacker calls `setMaxFeePercent(2 ether)`. The broken `onlyManager` modifier allows this. 2. Attacker calls `setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether)`. The total fees are now 1.1 ether (110%). 3. A victim calls `sellCurvesToken(subject, amount)`. 4. `sellCurvesToken` calls `_transferFees`. 5. `_transferFees` calls `getFees(price)`, returning fees that sum to 1.1 * price. 6. `_transferFees` attempts to calculate `sellValue = price - protocolFee - subjectFee - referralFee - holderFee`. 7. The calculation underflows (price - 1.1 * price) and reverts, blocking the sell.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The attack scenario is highly realistic and trivial to execute. The attacker only needs to send two transactions to manipulate the fee parameters. Once the fees are set above 100%, the entire protocol is frozen for sellers. The attacker could potentially hold the protocol hostage, or if they also manipulate the `protocolFeeDestination` (via the similarly broken `onlyOwner` modifier on `setProtocolFeePercent`), they could extract value from any ongoing buys while sells are blocked.
validation_result.impact_assessment: CRITICAL. This vulnerability allows a complete Denial of Service (DoS) on the core functionality of the protocol (selling tokens). All user funds currently locked in the bonding curves would become permanently inaccessible until the fees are lowered (which any other user could theoretically do, but the attacker could repeatedly front-run or back-run to maintain the lock).
recommended_mitigations: Fix the `onlyManager` and `onlyOwner` modifiers in `Security.sol` by adding `require(msg.sender == owner, "Not owner");` and `require(managers[msg.sender], "Not manager");`.
Add a hard upper bound check in `setMaxFeePercent` to ensure `maxFeePercent_ <= 1 ether`.
Add a similar hard upper bound check in `setProtocolFeePercent` and `setExternalFeePercent` to ensure the sum of all fees never exceeds 1 ether.
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy Curves contract. 2. As a non-owner/non-manager account, call `setMaxFeePercent(2 ether)`. 3. Call `setExternalFeePercent(0.5 ether, 0.3 ether, 0.3 ether)`. 4. Have another account buy some tokens via `buyCurvesToken`. 5. Have that account attempt to sell the tokens via `sellCurvesToken`. 6. Observe the transaction revert due to arithmetic underflow.
